narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the strength that brings to your journey. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, LSAT score of 40, first-year school decile of 1, third-year school decile of 1, and your plan to attend full-time—against patterns from thousands of past students who took the bar exam. It predicted a 56% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is just above the threshold that led to your application being rejected to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom group, as lower rankings like yours signal struggles that often correlate with bar exam difficulties compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their class. Your first-year decile in the bottom group also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance below the dataset average. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 40—higher than the average of about 37—strongly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, showing your potential in that area. Your low family income actually nudged things slightly toward passing, perhaps reflecting resilience common among students from similar backgrounds, while your full-time status and undergraduate GPA had minimal overall sway. Compared to typical students, your decile rankings stand out as much weaker than the middle-of-the-pack averages, while your LSAT is a bright spot above the norm. This combination tipped the scales toward rejection, even with those strengths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, seeking academic support to improve your school rankings in future studies, or exploring programs with strong bar prep for students from underrepresented backgrounds like yours. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing; your story matters.
